log_entries.thought_preview: The sample is a PE32 executable with GUI capabilities, and it has been analyzed in two different sandboxes. The tags suggest that it has runtime modules, persistence mechanisms, and an overlay.
The sample does not seem to have any network activity, but it has dropped two files and has used the SetWindowsHookExA API call. It also has two registry persistence keys.
The sample has created several notable modules, but they are all legitimate Windows system files. The highlighted API call, SetWindowsHookExA, is suspicious.

The sample has created two registry persistence keys, which suggests that it is attempting to maintain persistence on the system. The keys point to the explorer.exe and svchost.exe files, which are su
The sample has dropped two files, spoolsv.exe and svchost.exe, which are suspicious because they are not legitimate Windows system files. These files are likely used for persistence and execution.
The sample does not seem to have any network activity, which suggests that it may be a local-only malware. The lack of DNS lookups and HTTP conversations also supports this hypothesis.

The sample exhibits several interesting behaviors, including the ability to acquire debug privileges, contain a resource section, and check HTTP status codes. The fact that it was compiled from Visual

- Do not invent placeholder IDs such as T1234.
- Remove TTPs that are not directly justified by the report.
